sampleSummary.notes: Fixture for hierarchy-aware profiles. Not a real profile.

hierarchy.purpose: Dense research framing and contribution statements
hierarchy.notes: One abstract-like sample seeds the branch but needs more academic sections.

hierarchy.purpose: Polite professional follow-up with a clear ask

hierarchyNotes: Existing flat 0.2.0 profiles can omit these fields.
Use contextSlices only where samples show reusable differences.

tone.description: Clear and practical, with warmth when the context allows it.
evidence: Could we move this to Thursday morning?

sentenceRhythm.description: Mixes concise lead sentences with one explanatory sentence when needed.
evidence: The prototype makes interruptions visible before they become coordination debt.

paragraphStructure.description: Leads with the point, then adds one reason or next step.
evidence: Could we move this to Thursday morning? I need one more pass on the notes before we meet.

directness.description: States the request or contribution plainly.
evidence: Could we move this to Thursday morning?

evidenceStyle.description: Uses concrete artifacts or timing details rather than broad claims.
evidence: one more pass on the notes

transitions.description: Uses light causal links rather than formal transition stacks.
evidence: so I can send it before lunch

lexicon.description: Plain verbs and concrete nouns; avoids inflated praise.

hedging.description: Uses modest qualifiers when evidence is preliminary.

prefer: lead with the concrete ask or contribution
name the artifact or timing constraint
strategyNotes: Pick the matching context slice before borrowing rhythm or lexicon from other branches.

examples.generic: This work has the potential to drive meaningful impact across teams.
examples.inVoice: The prototype makes interruptions visible before they become coordination debt.
examples.rationale: Replaces broad impact language with a concrete mechanism.

limits: Do not fabricate publications, roles, or sample history not present in supplied evidence.

customBlocklist.reason: Too broad unless paired with a concrete mechanism.

evidenceStyle.description: Frames a problem, method, and contribution in sequence.

hedging.description: Uses cautious claims for preliminary evidence.

strategyNotes: Use this branch for abstracts and short research summaries only.

examples.generic: This paper explores an important and timely problem.
examples.inVoice: We study how interruptions become coordination debt in small research teams.
examples.rationale: Names the phenomenon instead of praising the topic.

tone.description: Polite and direct without decorative enthusiasm.
evidence: I wanted to follow up on the draft I sent Tuesday.

directness.description: Makes the next requested action explicit.
evidence: Could you send comments by Friday?

paragraphStructure.description: Two short paragraphs: context, then ask.
evidence: Could you send comments by Friday?

avoid: checking in with no concrete request
strategyNotes: Use for formal email follow-ups and first professional replies.

evidence: Thursday morning works better on my side.
Could we move this to Thursday morning? I need one more pass on the notes before we meet.

strategyNotes: Use for short informal coordination, not public social posts.

sources.title: Fixture abstract excerpt
sources.text: We study how interruptions become coordination debt in small research teams. We introduce a lightweight annotation workflow and show that early results suggest clearer handoffs after meetings.

sources.title: Fixture formal follow-up
sources.text: I wanted to follow up on the draft I sent Tuesday. Could you send comments by Friday so I can fold them into the next version?

sources.title: Fixture coordination reply
sources.text: Could we move this to Thursday morning? I need one more pass on the notes before we meet.
